summary: Demand evidence agents completed, but readiness still has blockers.

demand_status.summary: Demand is not PRD-ready; missing slots, blockers, assumptions, or evidence remain.

triage.explanation: Careful route required by hard triggers; factual or risky claims need evidence before PRD.

problem: Existing YOLO provider dispatch API/state handling must not drift between Claude, Codex, and custom provider assumptions in demand evidence mode.
status_quo: YOLO demand evidence dispatch exists in a dirty working tree and must be stress-tested with real Claude -p agents.
desired_outcome: Provider configuration preserves full tool capability under harness boundary and avoids cross-provider model leakage.
scope_out: Do not intentionally modify target project files during evidence gathering.
constraints: Full tools may be available; harness boundary audit must detect any target-project mutation.
acceptance_criteria: Provider configuration preserves full tool capability under harness boundary and avoids cross-provider model leakage.
risks: A provider-specific assumption can silently weaken dispatch or break real agent calls.

protocol.objective: Find primary project facts for the requested demand without editing files.
protocol.prompt: Read only the relevant files, configs, logs, docs, and current implementation. Return claims with exact paths/lines or source summaries. Separate assumptions from facts.

evidence_tasks.reason: Factual project claims need primary evidence.

protocol.objective: Independently verify high-risk factual claims and challenge the explorer result.
protocol.prompt: Use independent reads or sources. Confirm, weaken, or contradict each claim. Any conflict must become a blocker instead of being averaged away.
protocol.required_when: evidence_policy === cross_check or claim risk is high

protocol.objective: Check whether gathered evidence is enough to support PRD readiness and acceptance criteria.
protocol.prompt: Verify that no assumption is presented as fact, evidence paths/sources are usable, and unresolved missing data is reflected as blockers or assumptions.

evidence_tasks.reason: Verify blockers and assumptions are not promoted to facts.

state.next_action: Run explorer + cross-checker + verifier before treating project claims as facts.

actions.reason: Factual project claims need primary evidence.

protocol.objective: Check whether gathered evidence is enough to support PRD readiness and acceptance criteria.

agent_results.claim: YOLO evidence dispatch has provider-specific configuration paths and tool capability variations across Claude, Codex, and custom providers that require explicit normalization to prevent drift.

evidence.summary: executionConfig function normalizes provider selection with different logic for claude, codex, and custom providers
evidence.why: This is the core function that handles provider configuration during evidence dispatch and shows provider-specific paths

evidence.summary: Claude provider normalization clears settings, tools, and slash command flags then sets agent_tool_profile and no_session_persistence
evidence.why: Shows evidence mode explicitly removes Claude's configuration options to enforce boundary control

evidence.summary: Custom provider sets custom_sandbox to 'boundary' mode during evidence dispatch
evidence.why: Demonstrates custom provider configuration differs from Claude/Codex in evidence mode

evidence.summary: buildProviderInvocation creates provider-specific command structures: codex uses exec with output file, claude uses -p with tools/settings, custom uses shell execution
evidence.why: Shows fundamental differences in how each provider is invoked and what parameters they accept

evidence.summary: Codex invocation includes sandbox mode, approval policy, and output file capture for last message
evidence.why: Codex has different execution model with file-based output capture not used by Claude or custom

evidence.summary: Claude invocation supports permission mode, settings, tools, allowed/disallowed tools, slash commands disable, session persistence disable, and max budget
evidence.why: Claude has the most complex configuration options, many of which are cleared in evidence mode

evidence.summary: Provider aliases normalize anthropic→claude, openai→codex, shell/local→custom
evidence.why: Shows provider name normalization that happens before configuration

evidence.summary: buildAgentAdapterCapabilities defines different sandbox, file_write, and shell_exec capabilities per provider
evidence.why: Each provider has different capability contracts that affect what agents can do in evidence mode

evidence.summary: buildBoundarySnapshot and diffBoundarySnapshots detect project file mutations by comparing before/after file digests
evidence.why: Boundary enforcement system that prevents evidence agents from modifying project files

evidence.summary: runDemandEvidenceDispatchRuntime executes evidence agents with boundary checks and feeds results into PRD readiness
evidence.why: Main dispatch runtime that orchestrates provider execution with boundary enforcement

evidence.summary: Test verifies codex dispatch does not inherit claude default model or force read-only sandbox
evidence.why: Test evidence showing provider isolation requirements

evidence.summary: Test verifies claude dispatch clears settings and tools flags and relies on boundary enforcement
evidence.why: Test evidence showing Claude configuration normalization in evidence mode

evidence.summary: Test verifies full tool profile requires explicit authorization and defaults to boundary without allowFullAgentTools
evidence.why: Test evidence showing tool profile variations require authorization

evidence.summary: Test verifies boundary blocks project file mutation even when agent results pass
evidence.why: Test evidence showing boundary enforcement is independent of agent success

evidence.summary: Test verifies git-backed boundary detects untracked project mutations
evidence.why: Test evidence showing boundary detection works for both modified and new files

evidence.summary: Current configuration defaults to claude executor with claude-sonnet-4-6 model and settings-minimal.json
evidence.why: Shows current default configuration that evidence dispatch must handle correctly

evidence.summary: withExecutionConfig function shows model inheritance pattern: if provider is codex, sets both model and codex_model
evidence.why: Demonstrates cross-provider model leakage risk in configuration helpers

evidence.summary: execution_policy defines writes_business_code: false, agent_instruction: boundary_controlled, agent_tool_profile, and project_mutation: forbidden
evidence.why: Shows evidence dispatch policy constraints that all providers must respect

assumptions: Evidence agents respect the boundary rules enforced by the snapshot system
Provider-specific tool restrictions are correctly applied before agent execution
JSON repair mechanism handles all common Claude output format variations
risks: A provider-specific assumption in executionConfig could silently weaken dispatch or break real agent calls
Tool profile variations (boundary vs full vs research) might not be consistently applied across all three providers
Model inheritance logic in withExecutionConfig could leak Claude model to Codex or vice versa
Boundary enforcement might not catch all mutation vectors (symlinks, external processes, file descriptor abuse)
Provider invocation differences (Codex output file vs Claude stdout) could cause result parsing failures
Agent tool capability differences per provider could lead to inconsistent evidence gathering
missing: Verification that current Codex installation handles the evidence dispatch invocation correctly
Evidence that custom provider commands respect boundary constraints in practice
Verification that tool profile normalization works identically across all three providers
Testing of real Claude agent behavior with evidence mode prompts vs test mocks

result.notes: Primary evidence confirms provider-specific configuration paths exist and are explicitly normalized in evidence dispatch. Code shows boundary enforcement, tool profile variations, and provider isolation mechanisms are implemented. Missing items are operational verification, not architectural gaps.

agent_results.claim: The explorer's claim that 'provider isolation mechanisms are implemented' is weakened by evidence that Codex defaults to write-capable sandbox mode and custom providers retain file_write/shell_exec capabilities in evidence mode, relying solely on post-facto boundary enforcement rather than provider-level restrictions.

evidence.summary: Codex invocation defaults to 'workspace-write' sandbox mode when codex_sandbox is not set
evidence.why: Contradicts the assertion that Codex is restricted in evidence mode; it defaults to a write-capable mode

evidence.summary: Test verifies codex_sandbox is undefined in evidence mode, confirming no explicit sandbox restriction is set
evidence.why: Test evidence confirms that evidence dispatch does not override Codex's default write-capable sandbox

evidence.summary: Evidence dispatch sets custom_sandbox to 'boundary' for custom providers
evidence.why: Shows the configuration value set in evidence mode for custom providers

evidence.summary: Custom provider capability contract restricts file_write and shell_exec only when customMode is 'read-only', not 'boundary'
evidence.why: Demonstrates that 'boundary' mode does not prevent file writes or shell execution for custom providers

evidence.summary: file_write and shell_exec are true for custom providers unless mode is 'read-only'
evidence.why: Confirms custom providers retain write and shell capabilities in 'boundary' mode used by evidence dispatch

evidence.summary: Test verifies boundary enforcement blocks mutations after they occur, proving enforcement is post-facto not preventative
evidence.why: Evidence that boundary detection happens after execution, not at provider capability level

evidence.summary: Boundary snapshot is taken before agent execution and compared after, confirming post-facto detection model
evidence.why: Confirms the architecture relies on detecting violations after they happen rather than preventing them at provider level

risks: Codex agents in evidence mode can execute file writes because workspace-write sandbox is the default and not overridden
Custom provider agents in evidence mode can execute file writes and shell commands because 'boundary' mode does not restrict these capabilities
Boundary enforcement only detects mutations after they occur; it does not prevent them at the provider invocation level
A malicious or buggy evidence agent could exploit file_write capabilities to mutate project files before boundary detection runs
The explorer's conclusion that 'provider isolation mechanisms are implemented' overstated the actual preventative restrictions
missing: Verification that evidence mode prompts explicitly forbid file writes regardless of provider capability
Testing of Codex or custom provider agents actually attempting file mutations in evidence mode to verify boundary catches them
Evidence that provider-level sandbox restrictions (not just post-facto boundary checks) are considered out of scope or intentionally omitted

result.notes: The explorer's primary claim about provider-specific configuration paths is accurate, but the conclusion about provider isolation is weakened. Codex and custom providers retain write-capable defaults in evidence mode; boundary enforcement is post-facto detection, not provider-level prevention. This is an architectural distinction that should be reflected in the risk assessment, not a blocker to proceeding.

agent_results.claim: The gathered evidence from explorer and cross-checker is sufficient to support PRD readiness for provider-specific configuration normalization. Both agents properly separated facts from assumptions, provided usable evidence paths with line numbers, and identified missing operational verification as distinct from architectural gaps.

evidence.summary: executionConfig function with provider-specific normalization logic confirmed by both explorer and cross-checker
evidence.why: Primary fact supporting the claim about provider-specific configuration paths

evidence.summary: Provider invocation differences between codex (file-based output), claude (complex settings), and custom (shell execution) verified by explorer
evidence.why: Supports the claim that providers have fundamentally different execution models requiring normalization

evidence.summary: Codex defaults to workspace-write sandbox mode when codex_sandbox is not set, independently confirmed by cross-checker
evidence.why: Cross-checker evidence that weakens the explorer's 'provider isolation' claim and shows reliance on post-facto boundary enforcement

evidence.summary: Custom providers retain file_write and shell_exec capabilities in 'boundary' mode, independently verified by cross-checker
evidence.why: Cross-checker evidence showing boundary mode does not prevent writes at provider capability level

evidence.summary: Boundary enforcement tests verify post-facto detection of mutations after agent execution completes
evidence.why: Evidence from both agents confirming boundary enforcement architecture is detection-based, not prevention-based

evidence.summary: execution_policy defines project_mutation: forbidden and agent_instruction: boundary_controlled
evidence.why: Policy-level evidence that the system explicitly forbids project mutations in evidence mode

evidence.summary: Current default configuration uses claude executor with claude-sonnet-4-6 model
evidence.why: Contextual evidence showing what the evidence dispatch must handle correctly in the current environment

risks: The architectural distinction between provider-level prevention and post-facto boundary detection could be misinterpreted as a gap rather than a design choice
Cross-checker's weakening of the 'provider isolation' claim might be seen as contradictory to the explorer's 'pass' verdict when both are actually consistent
Missing operational verification items (Codex installation, custom provider behavior) might be treated as blockers when they are verification gaps not architectural gaps

result.notes: Explorer and cross-checker independently verified the core claim with specific, usable evidence paths. Cross-checker correctly identified a nuance: provider isolation relies on post-facto boundary detection rather than provider-level prevention, which both agents properly documented as evidence rather than assumption. Missing items are operational verification, not blockers to PRD readiness. The evidence is sufficient to proceed.

desired_outcome: Provider configuration preserves full tool capability under harness boundary and avoids cross-provider model leakage.
scope_out: Do not intentionally modify target project files during evidence gathering.
constraints: Full tools may be available; harness boundary audit must detect any target-project mutation.

blockers.message: explorer reported unresolved missing evidence: Verification that current Codex installation handles the evidence dispatch invocation correctly; Evidence that custom provider commands respect boundary constraints in practice; Verification that tool profile normalization works identically across all three providers; Testing of real Claude agent behavior with evidence mode prompts vs test mocks

cross-checker reported unresolved missing evidence: Verification that evidence mode prompts explicitly forbid file writes regardless of provider capability; Testing of Codex or custom provider agents actually attempting file mutations in evidence mode to verify boundary catches them; Evidence that provider-level sandbox restrictions (not just post-facto boundary checks) are considered out of scope or intentionally omitted
